title: Stripe-like Payment Gateway — System Design
description: Design a payment gateway merchants integrate to charge customers' cards. The themes are the PCI boundary (never touching raw card data), idempotent charge orchestration across a fraud check and an external acquirer, reliable webhooks, and ledger + payouts to merchants.

nodes.label: Merchant App
nodes.description: Calls the gateway API to charge customers; receives webhooks.

nodes.label: Client SDK / Hosted Fields
nodes.description: Collects card data in the browser, tokenizing it off the merchant server.

nodes.label: Gateway API
nodes.description: Public charge/refund API; enforces idempotency and auth.

nodes.label: Card Vault (PCI)
nodes.description: Tokenizes and stores card data inside the PCI boundary.

nodes.label: Charge Orchestrator
nodes.description: Drives the payment state machine across fraud, PSP, and ledger.

nodes.label: Charge / Payment Store
nodes.description: Authoritative charge records, states, and idempotency keys.

nodes.label: Risk / Fraud Service
nodes.description: Scores the charge; approve, decline, or review.

nodes.label: Acquirer / Card Network
nodes.description: Bank/network that actually authorizes and settles the card.

nodes.label: Ledger Service
nodes.description: Double-entry record of money owed to/by merchants.

nodes.label: Webhook Delivery
nodes.description: Reliably delivers signed event notifications to merchants.

nodes.label: Webhook Queue
nodes.description: At-least-once queue of events with retry/backoff.

nodes.label: Payout Service
nodes.description: Aggregates ledger balances and pays merchants on a schedule.

functional: Charge a customer's card (authorize + capture) via a simple API.
Notify merchants of outcomes via webhooks.
Maintain a ledger of merchant balances and pay out on schedule.
nonFunctional: Correctness over availability — never double-charge or lose a charge.
Idempotent API: a retried charge request charges at most once (exactly-once external effect via idempotency + durable state + reconciliation, not a generic distributed-systems guarantee).
PCI compliance: minimize the surface that touches raw card data.
Reliable webhooks: every event is eventually delivered (at-least-once).
Auditable: every money movement is recorded and reconcilable.

capacity.label: Charge semantics
capacity.note: Idempotency key + durable state + reconciliation — not a generic distributed-systems guarantee.

capacity.label: Sync charge latency
capacity.note: Dominated by the external acquirer authorize/capture call.

capacity.label: Async work
capacity.note: Decoupled from the sync path; runs on queues/jobs, not the request thread.

capacity.label: Webhook fan-out
capacity.note: charge.succeeded, refund.created, payout.paid, ... Retried with ~1.3x retry multiplier; merchants dedup.

capacity.label: Ledger writes
capacity.note: Write amplification: one charge => charge row + idempotency row + 1+ payment_attempt + 2–6 ledger entries + 1+ webhook_events & delivery_attempts + 1 reconciliation match. ~2k charges/sec fans out to 10k+ persisted rows/sec.

capacity.label: Payout cadence
capacity.note: Aggregate available ledger balance at a cutoff, one PSP transfer per merchant.

capacity.label: Retention & storage
capacity.note: Charges/ledger/payouts kept for audit/compliance; idempotency keys expire in ~24h. Tens of TB/year growth — motivates sharding the charge store and async queues.

api.description: Charge a tokenized card. Authenticated by merchant API key; idempotent on the Idempotency-Key header (same key + same body replays the original result, same key + different body returns 409).
Refund a charge (full or partial). Idempotent on the Idempotency-Key header; amount is capped at captured_amount - already_refunded, so over-refunding returns 400.
Fetch a charge's current state and history (lets merchants reconcile or recover after missing a webhook).
List recent webhook events for the merchant (poll/backfill if a webhook was missed).
Re-deliver a past event to the configured endpoint (recovery after an outage).
Configure a webhook endpoint: URL, subscribed event types, and retry policy. Returns the signing secret (rotatable, versioned).
Tokenize card data in the browser (hosted fields), keeping it off the merchant server.
api.request: { "card": { ... } }  (sent from client SDK to the vault)

dataModel.name: charges
dataModel.note: Authoritative charge record + state machine. State distinguishes sent-but-unknown external calls so an ambiguous PSP response is never blindly retried.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(created, risk_review, authorize_sent, authorize_unknown, authorized, capture_sent, capture_unknown, captured, succeeded, voided, refunded, declined, failed)

fields.name: psp_ref / acquirer_id
fields.type: external acquirer reference + which acquirer handled it

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: One row per external acquirer call. Holds the PSP-side idempotency key so a retry resolves the original attempt instead of creating a new authorization.

fields.name: psp_request_id / psp_idempotency_key
fields.type: varchar (sent to the PSP before the call)

fields.name: kind / status
fields.type: enum(authorize, capture, void, refund) / enum(sent, unknown, succeeded, failed)

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Per-merchant request dedup. Stores a request fingerprint so a repeat with the SAME key but a DIFFERENT body is a defined 409 conflict, not a silent second charge.

fields.name: charge_id / response_snapshot
fields.type: uuid / json (the original result replayed on repeat)

dataModel.name: refunds
dataModel.note: A refund is its own idempotent money movement against a captured charge; partial refunds are capped at captured_amount - refunded_amount.

dataModel.name: ledger_accounts
dataModel.note: Double-entry accounts. Each merchant has available and pending balances; the platform has fee and reserve accounts.

dataModel.name: ledger_entries
dataModel.note: Immutable debit/credit lines grouped into balanced transactions that net to zero. Reversals are new entries, never edits.

dataModel.name: payouts
dataModel.note: A scheduled settlement of a merchant's available balance, with its own state machine and idempotent PSP transfer.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(scheduled, sent, unknown, paid, failed, returned)

dataModel.name: reconciliation_runs
dataModel.note: Each ingest of a PSP settlement report and the matched/mismatched results that drive compensating ledger entries.

dataModel.name: card_tokens (vault)
dataModel.note: Inside the PCI boundary; merchants never see raw PAN.

dataModel.name: webhook_events (outbox)
dataModel.note: The immutable event record, written in the same transaction as the charge/refund/payout state change (transactional outbox). Merchants dedup on event_id.

dataModel.name: webhook_delivery_attempts
dataModel.note: One row per delivery try, separate from the event so retries/backoff/DLQ are auditable and replay is possible.

dataModel.name: merchants
dataModel.note: Merchant auth and per-merchant policy. API keys authenticate requests; rate limits are enforced per merchant.

dataModel.name: webhook_endpoints
dataModel.note: Per-merchant webhook configuration, including the signing secret (versioned for rotation) and retry policy.

fields.name: signing_secret / secret_version
fields.type: secret used to sign payloads / int

patterns.name: PCI tokenization boundary
patterns.what: Collect/store card data only in a small vault; everything else handles tokens.
patterns.whenToUse: Any system touching card data — shrink PCI scope.
patterns.group: The PCI Boundary: Tokenize Card Data

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: A merchant-supplied key dedups retried charge requests so a card is charged once.

patterns.name: Charge state machine
patterns.what: Model the charge as explicit states across risk, PSP, and ledger.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step money flows that must be consistent and resumable.

patterns.name: Risk scoring gate
patterns.what: Score the charge for fraud and approve/decline/review before hitting the network.

patterns.name: Reliable webhooks (outbox + retry)
patterns.what: Persist events and deliver at-least-once with retries, backoff, and signatures.
patterns.whenToUse: Notifying merchants of async outcomes durably.

patterns.name: Double-entry ledger + payouts
patterns.what: Record every money movement in a ledger; aggregate balances and pay merchants.
patterns.whenToUse: Tracking what's owed and settling it correctly.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Write the event in the same DB transaction as the state change, then a relay drains the outbox — so an event exists iff its transition committed.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever a state change must reliably emit an event without a lossy dual-write.

patterns.name: PSP reconciliation
patterns.what: Resolve ambiguous external calls and verify internal state against the PSP's authoritative status and settlement reports; correct drift with compensating entries.
patterns.whenToUse: Any system whose source of truth is an external provider that can time out or disagree.

patterns.name: Payout state machine
patterns.what: Model a payout as an idempotent, reconciled state machine (scheduled -> sent -> paid/returned), never a fire-and-forget transfer.
patterns.whenToUse: Sending money out where the transfer can fail, be unknown, or be returned.

patterns.name: Merchant webhook dedup
patterns.what: Consumers dedup by event id because delivery is at-least-once; the gateway never claims exactly-once delivery.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once event delivery to external consumers.

patterns.name: Circuit breaker + failover
patterns.what: Trip a breaker around a failing PSP and fail over to another acquirer only when it is safe (before authorize sent, or after a confirmed failure).
patterns.whenToUse: Hard dependency on a flaky external provider where blind retries are dangerous.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Merchant Server Charges the Bank Directly (the baseline)
description: Start with the obvious version: the merchant's server collects the raw card number in a form, posts it to the gateway API, which forwards the PAN straight to the acquirer/bank to charge it.

It 'works' for one happy-path charge, and it exposes the two problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, raw card data now flows through (and likely gets logged by) the merchant server and your API — a PCI compliance nightmare and a huge breach blast radius. Second, when the network times out and the merchant retries, the bank gets charged twice: there is nothing tying the retry to the original attempt, so the customer is double-charged.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: tokenize card data so raw PANs never touch general systems, and make charges idempotent so retries charge at most once.

view.caption: The merchant app posts a charge to the gateway API, which forwards the raw card straight to the acquirer/card network.

steps.decisionPrompt: The merchant posts the raw card number and the charge call times out, so they retry. What stops the customer being charged twice — and who is now in PCI scope?
whyNow: Sending raw cards straight to the bank is the intuitive first attempt; seeing it fail on both PCI scope and double-charging is exactly what motivates tokenization and idempotency as the first two real steps.

recap.after: A single charge works by passing the raw card number through to the bank.
recap.newRisk: Raw card data touches the merchant server and your API (PCI nightmare), and a retried charge double-charges the customer.

traps.trap: Letting the merchant server handle the raw card number and forwarding it to the bank.
traps.why: Every system that touches the PAN is in PCI scope and a breach target, and a bare forward-to-bank double-charges on retry.
traps.instead: Tokenize card data at the client/vault and require an idempotency key so a card is charged at most once.

steps.title: 2. The PCI Boundary: Tokenize Card Data
description: The first design decision is to never let raw card data touch the merchant's servers or your general systems. The client SDK (hosted fields) sends card data directly to a small, hardened vault, which returns a token; the merchant only ever handles the token.

This shrinks PCI scope to the vault: the rest of the gateway operates on tokens, dramatically reducing compliance burden and breach blast radius. The vault stores the encrypted PAN and exposes only non-sensitive display data (last4, brand).

Detokenization (token -> PAN) happens only inside the vault when calling the card network, never elsewhere. The vault is the hardened core: PANs encrypted with a KMS/HSM-backed key with scheduled rotation, least-privilege detokenization (only the orchestrator's PSP call path may detokenize, and it's audit-logged), network segmentation, and a token lifecycle that can revoke/expire tokens.

Tokenization is necessary but not sufficient: PANs must never leak through logs, traces, analytics pipelines, search indexes, error reports, or support tooling. Redaction at the logging boundary is part of staying in scope.

view.caption: The client SDK tokenizes the card directly with the PCI vault and gets a token back, so the merchant only sends that token to the gateway API.

options.name: Client SDK posts card straight to the vault (default)
options.description: Hosted-field SDK in the client posts card data straight to a hardened vault that returns a token, so raw PANs never touch the merchant or the general API.
pros: Raw card data never touches the merchant's servers or your general API — PCI scope shrinks to just the vault.
The merchant only ever handles an opaque token, slashing breach blast radius.
Hosted fields keep card entry inside your hardened, audited component.
cons: Requires embedding and maintaining a client SDK / hosted fields across web and mobile.
The vault is now a critical, highly-regulated dependency for every charge.
Merchants must integrate the tokenization flow rather than just POSTing a charge.

view.caption: The hosted-field SDK posts the card straight to the vault for a token, keeping raw PANs off the merchant and the gateway API.

options.name: Tokenize server-side in your API
options.description: The merchant POSTs raw card data to your API, which tokenizes it server-side and stores the encrypted PAN, putting the API itself inside PCI scope.
pros: Simplest merchant integration — they POST card data to one endpoint, no SDK.
Full control over the tokenization logic and storage in one place.
No client-side hosted-fields toolchain to ship.
cons: Raw PANs flow through (and may be logged by) your API, dragging the whole API into PCI scope.
A breach of the API now exposes card numbers, not just tokens.
Merchants posting cards to you may pull their servers into scope too.

view.caption: The merchant posts the raw card to the gateway API, which tokenizes it server-side via the vault, putting the API inside PCI scope.

options.name: Delegate tokenization to the PSP/network
options.description: Tokenization is delegated to the card network's or PSP's vault, which owns the PANs, so the gateway only ever holds network-issued tokens.
pros: You never store or transmit PANs at all — the card network's token vault owns them.
Network tokens can survive card reissuance, improving recurring-charge success.
Minimal PCI surface on your side.
cons: Hard dependency on a single PSP's tokenization, complicating multi-acquirer failover.
Tokens are PSP-scoped, so switching or adding processors means re-tokenizing.
Less control over the token lifecycle and display metadata.

view.caption: The SDK exchanges the card for a network token at the acquirer/card network, so the gateway only handles network-issued tokens.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you charge a card without your servers (or the merchant's) ever storing the raw card number?

concepts.definition: Capturing card data in a small vault and handing out a token used everywhere else.
concepts.whyItMatters: Minimizes PCI scope and breach risk; merchants never see the PAN.
concepts.example: SDK -> vault -> tok_9; the merchant charges with tok_9.
concepts.group: The PCI Boundary: Tokenize Card Data

concepts.definition: KMS/HSM-backed encryption with key rotation, least-privilege + audited detokenization, network segmentation, and token lifecycle management.
concepts.whyItMatters: Tokenization only shrinks scope if the vault itself is hardened and the rest of the system can't leak PANs.
concepts.example: Only the orchestrator's PSP-call path may detokenize; every detokenize is logged and rate-limited.
concepts.group: The PCI Boundary: Tokenize Card Data

whyNow: The PCI boundary is the defining architectural constraint of a card gateway; establishing tokenization first shapes everything downstream.

traps.trap: Tokenizing the card but still logging or storing the PAN elsewhere.
traps.why: PANs that land in application logs, traces, analytics, search indexes, error reports, or support tools pull those systems right back into PCI scope and become a breach target.
traps.instead: Redact at the logging boundary, never put PANs in events/metrics, and keep detokenization confined to the vault's PSP-call path.

recap.before: Raw card passed to the bank; no PCI isolation.
recap.after: Card data isolated in a hardened vault; the system runs on tokens.
recap.newRisk: Charge requests over flaky networks get retried — a card must not be charged twice.

steps.title: 3. Idempotent Charges
description: Networks retry, and merchants retry on timeouts. Without protection, a retried charge double-charges the customer. The API requires an idempotency key per charge; the gateway records key -> result and returns the original result on any repeat.
Persist the idempotency key in the same transaction that creates the charge, so a crash can't leave a charge without its dedup record. This turns at-least-once requests into exactly-once charges.

Keys are scoped per merchant and stored durably; a repeat of an in-flight key returns the in-progress/final result rather than starting a new charge. Store a fingerprint (hash) of the request body alongside the key so a repeat of the same key with a different body is a defined 409 conflict, not an ambiguous second charge. Keys carry a retention horizon (e.g. 24h) after which they expire.

view.caption: The merchant's charge with an idempotency key flows through the gateway API to the charge orchestrator, which persists charge and dedup record together in the store.

options.name: Idempotency-key record in the charge transaction (default)
options.description: Each charge carries a merchant-supplied idempotency key whose key-to-result mapping is written in the same transaction as the charge, and repeats return the original result.
pros: Persist the key -> result mapping in the same transaction that creates the charge, so a crash can't leave a charge without its dedup record.
A repeat of the same key returns the original result, turning at-least-once requests into exactly-once charges.
Keys scoped per merchant and stored durably — clean, auditable contract.
cons: Merchants must generate and send a stable key per charge intent.
The key store grows and needs a retention/expiry policy.
Concurrent retries of the same key race on first insert and need careful handling.

view.caption: The orchestrator writes the idempotency key's result alongside the charge in one transaction in the payment store, returning the original on repeats.

options.name: Unique constraint on (merchant, key)
options.description: A unique index on (merchant, key) on the charge table rejects a duplicate insert atomically, and the caller then fetches and returns the original charge.
pros: Lean on the database: a unique index rejects a duplicate charge insert atomically.
No separate idempotency table to keep consistent with the charge row.
Race-safe by construction — the second concurrent insert simply fails.
cons: A constraint violation only says 'duplicate', so you must still fetch and return the original result.
Awkward when the request body differs but the key repeats — needs a conflict policy.
Couples dedup to the charge table's schema and write path.

view.caption: The orchestrator inserts the charge under a unique (merchant, key) constraint in the store, so a duplicate insert is rejected atomically.

options.name: Distributed lock per key
options.description: A short-lived distributed lock on the key (e.g. in Redis) serializes retries so only one charge proceeds, keeping the dedup decision out of the database write path.
pros: A short-lived lock on the key serializes retries so only one charge proceeds.
Keeps the dedup decision out of the primary database's write path.
Conceptually simple: acquire, charge, release.
cons: Lock expiry vs a slow PSP call risks a second holder double-charging.
Adds a lock service (e.g. Redis) as another dependency on the critical path.
Crashes mid-lock need careful fencing to avoid stuck or duplicated charges.

view.caption: The orchestrator takes a per-key lock from a lock service to serialize retries before persisting the charge to the store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A merchant's charge call times out and they retry. How do you guarantee the customer is charged once?

concepts.definition: A client key the gateway uses to dedup retried charge requests.
concepts.example: Retry with Idempotency-Key k_1 returns ch_9, not a second charge.

whyNow: Idempotency is the second payments non-negotiable; doing it transactionally with charge creation is the correctness nuance.

traps.trap: Accepting a repeated idempotency key without comparing the request body.
traps.why: If the merchant reuses a key for a different amount or card, returning the old result (or charging again) is silently wrong.
traps.instead: Fingerprint the request body; same key + same body replays the result, same key + different body is a 409 conflict.

traps.trap: Caching idempotency in Redis only, not in the durable charge transaction.
traps.why: A cache eviction or restart loses the dedup record and a retry double-charges.
traps.instead: Persist key -> result in the same transaction as the charge; treat any cache as an optimization, not the source of truth.

recap.after: Each charge request charges at most once.
recap.newRisk: A charge is multi-step (risk, authorize, capture, ledger) — it must stay consistent across them.

steps.title: 4. Charge Orchestration: Risk -> Network
description: A charge is a state machine with explicit states for sent-but-unknown external calls: created -> risk_review -> authorize_sent -> authorized -> capture_sent -> captured -> succeeded, with authorize_unknown/capture_unknown branches when the PSP times out, plus voided/declined/failed. The charge orchestrator drives it, persisting each transition so a crash resumes rather than re-charges.

First, the risk/fraud service scores the charge and approves, declines, or flags for review — stopping bad charges before they hit the network (reducing fraud and chargebacks). Approved charges go to the acquirer/card network to authorize and capture, with the result recorded.

The external PSP call is the slow, failure-prone step. Before the network call, write a payment_attempt row carrying a psp_request_id and a psp_idempotency_key, then move the charge to authorize_sent; on a timeout the charge sits in authorize_unknown and is reconciled via the PSP's status (or replayed safely with the same PSP idempotency key), never blindly retried — so you don't double-authorize.

view.caption: The charge orchestrator scores risk with the fraud service, detokenizes via the vault, authorizes/captures at the acquirer, and persists each transition to the store.

options.name: Persisted state-machine orchestrator (default)
options.description: A central orchestrator drives the charge state machine — created, risk_review, authorized, captured, succeeded — persisting each transition so a crash resumes rather than re-charges.
pros: Each transition (created -> risk_review -> authorize_sent -> authorized -> capture_sent -> captured -> succeeded) is persisted, so a crash resumes rather than re-charges.
One orchestrator owns the flow, making the charge lifecycle easy to reason about and audit.
Network timeouts land the charge in authorize_unknown/capture_unknown; the stored psp_request_id/psp_idempotency_key lets it reconcile or safely replay the same attempt.
cons: A central orchestrator is one more service to scale and make highly available.
Defining and migrating the state machine adds upfront design work.
Long-running flows hold state that must be stored and cleaned up.

view.caption: A persisted-state orchestrator drives risk, vault detokenization, and acquirer authorize/capture, recording each transition in the payment store.

options.name: Event-driven choreography (no central orchestrator)
options.description: Each service reacts to events (risk.approved authorizes, authorized captures) and emits its own, so the charge flow runs across services with no central coordinator.
pros: Each service reacts to events (risk.approved -> authorize, authorized -> capture) with no central coordinator to bottleneck.
Services stay loosely coupled and independently deployable.
Naturally resilient — a slow consumer just lags, it doesn't block the others.
cons: The overall charge lifecycle is spread across services, making it hard to see and debug.
Money flows demand strict ordering and exactly-once handling, which is harder without one owner.
Adding a step means changing event contracts across multiple services.

view.caption: The orchestrator emits charge events to an event bus that triggers the fraud service and acquirer with no central coordinator.

options.name: Synchronous inline calls in one request
options.description: Risk, authorize, and capture are called inline in a single synchronous request handler that returns the final outcome to the merchant in one response.
pros: Simplest to write: call risk, then authorize, then capture in a single request handler.
The merchant gets the final outcome in one synchronous response.
No state machine or event plumbing to build.
cons: A crash or timeout mid-sequence leaves the charge in an unknown state with no resume point.
A timed-out authorize can't be safely retried without risking a double charge.
Ties up a request thread through slow external network calls.

view.caption: The merchant's blocking call drives the orchestrator to call risk and the acquirer inline and return the final outcome in one response.

steps.decisionPrompt: You send an authorize to the card network and the response times out. Did it go through? What do you do?

concepts.definition: Explicit charge states with safe handling of ambiguous external (PSP) responses.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the charge consistent across a slow external dependency without double-authorizing.
concepts.example: Timeout on authorize -> query PSP status -> resolve to authorized or retry safely.

whyNow: Orchestration across risk + external network is the heart of the gateway; ambiguous-PSP-response handling is the senior signal.

recap.after: A consistent, resumable multi-step charge across risk and the network.
recap.newRisk: The merchant needs to know the outcome — reliably, even for async settlement events.

traps.why: The first one may have succeeded; retrying double-authorizes the card.
traps.instead: Reconcile via the PSP's authoritative status before any retry; idempotency keys to the PSP where supported.

flows.name: Charge orchestration: risk -> authorize (with timeout reconcile)
flows.note: Risk gates before the network; an ambiguous authorize timeout is reconciled via the PSP's status rather than blindly retried.

steps.title: 5. Reliable Webhooks
description: Outcomes are often async (a capture settles later; the network sends events). Merchants learn results via webhooks, which must be reliable: persist each event and deliver it at-least-once with retries and exponential backoff.

Use the transactional outbox — write the event in the same transaction as the charge state change, then a delivery service streams it — so an event exists iff its charge transition committed. Sign payloads so merchants can verify authenticity, and include an event id so merchants dedup (delivery is at-least-once).

Keep the immutable event record separate from per-delivery attempts so retries, backoff, and DLQ are auditable. Each merchant configures an endpoint URL, subscribed event types, a versioned signing secret (so secrets can be rotated without downtime), and a retry policy. Failed deliveries retry with backoff and dead-letter after a max-attempts/backoff limit (merchant endpoint down); merchants can list (GET /v1/events) and replay (POST /v1/events/{id}/replay) to catch up.

view.caption: The orchestrator emits an event to the webhook delivery service, which enqueues it and delivers a signed webhook to the merchant, with the acquirer feeding async settlement notices.

options.name: Transactional outbox + delivery service (default)
options.description: The event is written to an outbox table in the same transaction as the charge transition, and a delivery service streams the outbox and retries with backoff.
pros: Write the event in the same transaction as the charge state change, so an event exists iff its transition committed.
A delivery service streams the outbox and retries with exponential backoff for at-least-once delivery.
No lost or phantom events even if the app crashes right after the DB commit.
cons: Requires an outbox table and a relay/CDC process to drain it.
Adds delivery latency versus pushing the webhook inline.
Merchants must dedupe on event id since delivery is at-least-once.

view.caption: The orchestrator's event is queued by the webhook delivery service and retried to the merchant, while the acquirer feeds async settlement notices.

options.name: Push the webhook inline after the charge
options.description: An HTTP POST to the merchant fires inline right after the charge commits, with no outbox or delivery service buffering the event.
pros: Simplest path: fire the HTTP POST to the merchant right after committing the charge.
Lowest delivery latency on the happy path.
No outbox table or delivery service to operate.
cons: If the app crashes after commit but before the POST, the event is lost forever.
A merchant endpoint that is down for an hour means the event is simply dropped.
Dual-write (DB then HTTP) has no atomicity, so DB and merchant can disagree.

view.caption: The orchestrator POSTs the webhook to the merchant inline right after the charge commits, with no queue buffering it.

options.name: Merchants poll a charge-status API
options.description: No events are pushed; merchants pull current charge state from a status API whenever they want it, catching up after any downtime on their next poll.
pros: No delivery infrastructure — merchants pull current state when they want it.
Robust to merchant downtime: they just catch up on their next poll.
Simple, stateless server side.
cons: Polling adds latency and wastes requests when nothing has changed.
Real-time-ish updates require frequent polling, which doesn't scale across many merchants.
Shifts reliability and timing burden onto every merchant integration.

view.caption: The merchant polls the gateway API for current charge state, which reads it from the payment store, with no pushed events.

steps.decisionPrompt: A merchant's webhook endpoint is down for an hour. How do you make sure they still get the charge.succeeded event?

concepts.definition: Outbox-persisted events delivered at-least-once with retries, backoff, and signatures.
concepts.whyItMatters: Merchants must learn async outcomes durably; signatures + event ids give authenticity and dedup.
concepts.example: charge.succeeded retried until 2xx; merchant verifies signature and dedups by event id.

whyNow: Reliable async notification is a core gateway feature and a classic at-least-once + idempotency story on the consumer side.

traps.why: Delivery over the network is at-least-once; a 2xx that the merchant never recorded, or a retry after a slow ack, both cause duplicates.
traps.instead: Deliver at-least-once with a stable event id and tell merchants to dedup by event id; never claim exactly-once.

traps.trap: Dual-writing the DB then firing the HTTP POST inline.
traps.why: A crash between commit and POST loses the event; the merchant and gateway then disagree with no recovery.
traps.instead: Use the transactional outbox: persist the event with the state change, and let a relay deliver and retry it.

recap.newRisk: Money owed to merchants must be tracked and actually paid out.

flows.name: Reliable webhook delivery (at-least-once, retry/DLQ)
flows.note: Events are persisted then delivered at-least-once; a failed delivery retries with backoff, dead-lettering after a limit.

messages.label: retry with backoff; DLQ after limit

steps.title: 6. Ledger and Payouts
description: Every charge, refund, and fee is a money movement recorded in a double-entry ledger — the auditable source of truth for what the platform owes each merchant (and what it collected in fees). Charges credit a merchant's balance; refunds and fees debit it.

Payouts run on a schedule: aggregate each merchant's available ledger balance and send the payout via the network, recording it as a ledger entry too. The ledger reconciles against the PSP's settlement reports.
This reuses the double-entry + idempotency + reconciliation discipline of a money ledger — correctness over availability, immutable entries, balances derived and verified.

view.caption: The orchestrator posts entries to the ledger service, and the payout service reads balances from it to send payouts via the acquirer.

options.name: Double-entry ledger (default)
options.description: Every charge, refund, fee, and payout is recorded as balanced debit/credit entries in a double-entry ledger whose entries are immutable and must net to zero.
pros: Every charge, refund, and fee is a balanced money movement — the auditable source of truth for what each merchant is owed.
Debits and credits must net to zero, catching accounting bugs early.
Reconciles cleanly against the PSP's settlement reports.
cons: More schema and discipline than a single balance field.
Computing a current balance means summing entries (or maintaining a materialized balance).
Reversals/refunds must be modeled as new entries, never edits.

view.caption: The orchestrator posts balanced double-entry entries to the ledger, and the payout service reads those balances to send payouts via the acquirer.

options.name: Single mutable balance per merchant
options.description: Each merchant has a single balance column that is incremented on charge and decremented on refund, fee, and payout, with no per-movement history.
pros: Dead simple: increment on charge, decrement on refund/fee/payout.
Reading a balance is a single column lookup.
Minimal storage and easy to start with.
cons: No audit trail — you can't explain how a balance got to its value or reconstruct history.
Reconciliation against PSP settlements is hard with no per-movement record.
Concurrent updates to one row risk lost updates and disputes you can't resolve.

view.caption: The orchestrator increments/decrements a single mutable balance table that the payout service reads and debits.

options.name: Event-sourced ledger
options.description: Money movements are stored as an append-only event log, and balances are projections rebuilt from that log rather than posted ledger entries.
pros: Store an append-only log of money-movement events; balances are projections rebuilt from the log.
Complete, immutable history makes audit and time-travel queries trivial.
New views (per-fee, per-currency reports) are just new projections of the same events.
cons: Querying a current balance requires a maintained projection, adding read-model machinery.
More conceptual overhead than a posted double-entry ledger for most teams.
Schema/event versioning over years of history needs careful migration.

view.caption: The orchestrator appends money movements to an event log that projects balances the payout service reads.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you track what each merchant is owed and pay them correctly, including refunds and fees?

concepts.definition: Double-entry records of charges/refunds/fees aggregated into scheduled merchant payouts.
concepts.whyItMatters: Auditable, reconcilable money tracking and correct settlement.
concepts.example: Charge credits merchant balance minus fee; daily payout debits the balance.

concepts.definition: Funds sit in a pending balance until settled, move to available, and may be held in a reserve before they can be paid out.
concepts.whyItMatters: Paying out unsettled or reserved funds risks negative balances when refunds/chargebacks land.
concepts.example: A $20 charge credits pending; on settlement it moves to available; the daily payout pays available minus reserve.

whyNow: The ledger + payouts close the money loop; tying it to the double-entry discipline shows the whole financial flow is correct and auditable.

recap.newRisk: Volume spikes, PSP outages, and webhook fan-out stress the system.

failureDrills.scenario: The PSP settlement report disagrees with internal ledger entries.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reconciliation flags the mismatch; compensating ledger entries correct it; the report is authoritative for settled funds.
failureDrills.mitigation: Ledger reconciliation against PSP reports + compensating entries (never edit history).

flows.name: Refund: PSP reversal -> ledger reversal -> webhook
flows.note: A refund is its own idempotent state machine; it posts reversing (not edited) ledger entries and emits refund.created at-least-once.

flows.name: Scheduled payout: balance cutoff -> transfer -> reconcile -> webhook
flows.note: The payout worker snapshots available balance at a cutoff, sends an idempotent transfer, and only marks paid after reconciliation against the PSP.

messages.label: read available balance at cutoff (minus reserve)

steps.title: 7. Resilience, PSP Failures, and Scale
description: The gateway depends on external networks, so design for their failure: multiple acquirers with failover, circuit breakers around PSP calls, and queue-based retry for transient errors — while never double-charging (idempotency + reconciliation).

Scale the charge path horizontally (stateless orchestrator, charges sharded by id); webhook delivery and payouts are async consumers that scale independently. Spikes (holiday sales) buffer in queues; the charge state machine persists progress so nothing is lost on restart.
Everything money-touching is auditable and reconcilable; the ledger and charge store are authoritative, while webhook delivery state and risk caches are recoverable.

view.caption: The gateway API drives the stateless orchestrator, which persists to the sharded charge store, calls the acquirer behind a breaker, and hands events to the webhook delivery service to enqueue async.

options.name: Charge store: merchant-sharded primary (default)
options.description: Scale the charge/payment store by sharding on merchant_id with a strongly-consistent primary per shard, keeping each merchant's money records on one shard for clean idempotency and ledger reads.
pros: Horizontal write capacity for the ~10k+ rows/sec the money model fans out to.
A merchant's charges, idempotency keys, and ledger entries co-locate, so dedup and balance reads stay single-shard and strongly consistent.
Blast radius of a shard incident is limited to its merchants.
cons: Cross-merchant or global reporting must scatter-gather.
Hot merchants (huge sellers) can create skew that needs sub-sharding.
Resharding is operationally heavy and must preserve money invariants.

view.caption: The stateless orchestrator writes to a charge store sharded by merchant_id, keeping each merchant's money records on one strongly-consistent shard.

options.name: Charge store: single regional primary
options.description: Keep one strongly-consistent primary in one region with read replicas, deferring sharding until volume forces it.
pros: Simplest to reason about: one source of truth, trivial cross-merchant queries.
Strong consistency everywhere with no shard-routing logic.
Fine until you approach the write ceiling of a single primary.
cons: Single write ceiling becomes the throughput limit under peak.
Regional outage takes the whole gateway down.
Eventually forces a painful migration to sharding.

view.caption: All charges write to a single regional primary store with read replicas — simple but ceiling-bound.

options.name: Charge store: multi-region active/passive
options.description: Run a hot standby region that replicates the charge store and can take over on a regional failure, trading some failover lag for disaster resilience.
pros: Survives a full regional outage with a bounded RPO/RTO.
Keeps a strongly-consistent primary (active) while the passive only serves reads/standby.
Clear story for compliance/availability requirements.
cons: Cross-region replication lag means a failover can lose the most recent unreplicated writes unless synchronous (which adds latency).
Most expensive option to run and to test.
Failover drills are mandatory or the standby rots.

view.caption: The active primary replicates to a passive standby in another region that takes over on regional failure.

steps.decisionPrompt: Your primary acquirer goes down during peak. What happens to in-flight and new charges?
whyNow: Closing on PSP failure handling + scale shows the gateway stays correct and available despite external dependencies — the reliability requirement.

concepts.definition: Failing a charge over to another acquirer only when it is provably safe: before any authorize was sent, after a confirmed failure, or when a provider/network idempotency contract guarantees no double-authorization.
concepts.whyItMatters: Retrying an ambiguous authorize through a second acquirer is how you double-charge a customer.
concepts.example: authorize_unknown on acquirer A => reconcile A first; only route to B once A is confirmed failed.

concepts.definition: The money-specific metrics and alerts a gateway must watch beyond generic latency/error rates.
concepts.whyItMatters: These are the signals that catch silent money loss, stuck flows, and accounting drift before customers or auditors do.
concepts.example: Charge success rate, PSP timeout/unknown rate, webhook backlog age, reconciliation mismatch count, payout failure count, and ledger imbalance alerts (debits != credits).

traps.trap: Retrying a timed-out (ambiguous) authorize through a different acquirer.
traps.why: The first acquirer may have authorized; sending the same authorization to a second one double-charges the customer.
traps.instead: Reconcile the first attempt to a confirmed outcome before any failover; only route to another acquirer once the first is provably failed (or with a network-level idempotency contract).

traps.trap: Scaling the ledger with eventual consistency on balances that determine payouts.
traps.why: If a payout reads a stale or not-yet-reconciled balance, you can overpay a merchant and create a negative balance you can't recover.
traps.instead: Keep balance reads for payouts strongly consistent and reconciled; pay out available (settled) balance minus reserve, never pending.

recap.after: Multi-acquirer failover, circuit breakers, sharded scale, reconcilable state.
recap.newRisk: Acquirer failover and reconciliation lag — bounded by idempotency, circuit breakers, and authoritative ledgers.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Circuit breaker + acquirer failover + async retry for transient declines.

bottlenecks.issue: Webhook fan-out to slow merchant endpoints.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-endpoint queues, backoff, DLQ; don't let one slow merchant block others.

flows.name: PSP resilience: breaker + safe acquirer failover
flows.note: A tripped breaker on acquirer A only fails over after the in-flight charge is reconciled to a confirmed failure — never while it is ambiguous.

messages.label: resolve from A; do NOT route to B

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Payment Gateway
finalDesign.description: Card data is tokenized in a small PCI vault (client SDK -> vault), so the gateway runs on tokens. Charges are idempotent (merchant key, persisted with the charge) and driven by an orchestrator through a state machine: risk-score, then authorize/capture via an acquirer with careful ambiguous-response reconciliation (no double-auth). Outcomes flow to merchants via signed, at-least-once webhooks (transactional outbox + retry/DLQ). Every movement posts to a double-entry ledger that's reconciled against PSP settlement and drives scheduled merchant payouts. The charge path scales statelessly (sharded), external PSP calls use circuit breakers + multi-acquirer failover, and the ledger/charge store stay authoritative and auditable.

view.caption: Cards tokenize at the PCI vault via the SDK; the gateway API drives an idempotent orchestrator through risk, vault detokenization, and acquirer authorize/capture, posts to the ledger feeding payouts, and emits queued signed webhooks to the merchant.

functional.how: Idempotent refund (capped at remaining captured amount): PSP reversal, reversing ledger entries, refund.created webhook.
Risk scoring gate before the network.

nonFunctional.how: Idempotency key (+ request fingerprint) + explicit sent/unknown states + PSP reconciliation; exactly-once external effect, not exactly-once messaging.
Circuit breakers + safe multi-acquirer failover (only after reconcile) + sharded charge store.

say: Establish correctness over availability and exactly-once charges.
Pin PCI scope minimization and reliable webhooks as hard goals.
Note the external acquirer is the slow, fallible dependency.
Tokenize card data in a PCI vault.
Idempotent charge API + charge state machine.
Risk score, then authorize/capture via the acquirer.
Ledger + payouts; PSP failover and scale.
Tradeoffs: sync vs async capture, fraud strictness vs conversion, webhook retry policy.
Mention disputes/chargebacks and multi-currency as extensions.

expectations: Tokenizes cards and charges via a PSP.
May miss PSP-timeout reconciliation and reliable webhooks.
Designs the charge state machine + risk gate.
Discusses PCI scope, reconciliation, and chargebacks deeply.

followUps: How do you resolve an ambiguous authorize (timeout) without double-charging?
How would you handle disputes/chargebacks end to end (evidence submission, reserves, ledger impact)? — scoped out of the core case here.
How do you keep PCI scope minimal across the whole system?
How would you support multiple acquirers and route charges among them?
How do you make webhooks both reliable and not overwhelm a slow merchant?
Where would 3DS/SCA (asynchronous customer authentication) fit into the charge state machine? — out of scope above.
How would multi-currency and FX change the ledger (per-currency accounts, conversion entries)? — out of scope above.
How would merchant KYC/onboarding and bank-account verification gate payouts? — out of scope above.
